walkthrough.title: Place the Watering Hole
walkthrough.text: Let's get Nature set up! First, find the watering hole board. This is the central piece that represents the only place animals can eat plants. Place it in the middle of the table where everyone can reach it. Everything revolves around this watering hole, so give it some breathing room.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Size Dials
walkthrough.text: Now grab the size dials. These are the round dials that track how big each species is, from size 1 up to size 4. One side shows the forager icon and the other side shows the hunter icon. Place all the size dials on opposite sides of the table so everyone can reach them. You will take a new one each round when you get a new species.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Population Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the population tokens. These little wooden pieces represent individual animals in your species. Place them next to the size dials in a shared supply. Each round, every player will take one population token for their new species, and sometimes more if they lost population the previous round.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Food Supply
walkthrough.text: Next, set out the food trays near the watering hole. There are two types of food in Nature: plant food and meat food. Plants come from the watering hole when species forage. Meat comes from the supply when hunters successfully hunt. Make sure both types are easily accessible to all players.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Nature Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the Nature card deck. These cards have 8 different trait types that give your species superpowers. Give the deck a thorough shuffle and place it face-down near the watering hole. This is the Nature draw deck. Leave space next to it for a face-up discard pile that will form during play.

walkthrough.title: Place the Hunter Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the Hunter cards and place them in a stack near the watering hole. These are special free-action cards that any player can grab during the Adapting Phase to turn one of their species into a carnivore. Unlike Nature cards, Hunter cards are not drawn from the deck. They are always available.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Screens
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player screen. These screens hide the food tokens you collect each round. At the end of each round, you will place the food eaten by your population behind your screen. This food represents your score, and it stays hidden until the end of the game, which keeps things tense and exciting.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Now for the fun part. The person who can make the most amazing animal noise goes first! Give them the snow leopard first-player marker. This is one of the coolest first-player tokens in board gaming. The first player will change each round, passing to the left.

walkthrough.title: Determine Food Per Round
walkthrough.text: This is an important step. Draw the top card of the Nature deck and look at the food number in the bottom right corner. Multiply that number by the number of players. For example, with 3 players: if the card shows a 3, you get 3 times 3 equals 9 plants per round. Tuck the card under the watering hole as a reminder. For your first game, the rulebook recommends using a card with a food number of 3.

walkthrough.title: Food Reference Chart
walkthrough.text: Here is a handy reference for how much plant food goes into the watering hole each round based on card number and player count. With 2 players: card 1 gives 2, card 2 gives 4, card 3 gives 6, card 4 gives 8. With 3 players: card 1 gives 3, card 2 gives 6, card 3 gives 9, card 4 gives 12. With 4 players: card 1 gives 4, card 2 gives 8, card 3 gives 12, card 4 gives 16. The higher the number, the more food available, which means less starvation.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text: Setup is done! Each player should now have a player screen and nothing else in front of them. The watering hole is in the center with food trays, the Nature deck, and Hunter cards nearby. The size dials and population tokens are within reach. You are about to enter the world of Nature, where your species will evolve, feed, and struggle for survival over four rounds. Let's learn how to play!

summary.title: Ready to Play!
bullets: Each player has a player screen
Watering hole centered with food trays, Nature deck, and Hunter cards
Size dials and population tokens within reach
Game plays over 4 rounds

walkthrough.title: Game Overview and Goal
walkthrough.text: Nature is played over four rounds. Your goal is to grow your population and keep your animals alive. You score 1 point for every plant and every meat your population eats each round. At the end of the game, you also score 2 bonus points for each surviving species. The player with the most points wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most traits on their surviving species wins. If it is still tied, order two pizzas and play again!

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each of the four rounds has the same structure. First comes Preparation, where you add food to the watering hole, get a new species, and draw cards. Then comes the Adapting Phase, where each player gets one turn to play cards and evolve their species. Next is the Feeding Phase, where players take turns feeding their species until every population is fed, starved, or hunted. Finally comes Scoring, where you collect your food tokens behind your screen. Then you pass the snow leopard to the left and start a new round.

walkthrough.title: Preparation Phase
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, three things happen. First, add plants to the watering hole equal to the amount determined during setup. These are in addition to any leftover plants from last round. Second, every player takes a new species: a size dial set to 1 (forager side up) and one population token. If you lost population last round, add those lost population to your new species. If a species went extinct, add its size to your new species too. Third, every player draws 5 cards from the Nature deck. There is no hand limit, so these add to any cards you kept.

walkthrough.title: Adapting Phase: Playing Cards
walkthrough.text: Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player gets one turn during the Adapting Phase. On your turn, you can play as many cards as you want, in any order. Each card from your hand can be used for one of three actions: discard a card to increase a species' population by 1, discard a card to increase a species' size by 1 (up to the maximum of 4), or play a card face-down as a trait on a species (maximum 3 traits per species). You can mix and match these actions freely.

walkthrough.title: Adapting Phase: Free Actions
walkthrough.text: During your Adapting Phase turn, you also get free actions that do not cost a card. You can take a Hunter card and play it as a trait on any species, which flips its size dial to the hunter side. That species can no longer forage for plants. It hunts instead. You can also remove traits from your species to make room for different ones. And you can choose to hold cards in your hand for the Feeding Phase or the next round. When all players have taken their turn, flip all face-down traits face-up. The Adapting Phase is over.

walkthrough.title: Traits and Species Types
walkthrough.text: Traits are superpowers you play on species. Each species can have up to 3 traits. Traits can add to a species' hunt value (red tab) or defense value (grey tab). Base hunt and defense values equal the number on the size dial. There are also trigger traits marked with an exclamation point that activate automatically when a condition is met, like Scavenging (eat 2 meat when any species hunts), Opportunistic (eat 2 meat when one of your species forages or hunts, if plants remain), and Tusked (eat 1 meat when the watering hole becomes empty). Trigger traits are mandatory. A species without a Hunter card is a Herbivore. Add a trigger trait and it becomes an Omnivore. Add a Hunter card and it becomes a Carnivore.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Phase: Overview
walkthrough.text: The Feeding Phase is where the real action happens. Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player takes turns feeding one species at a time. On your turn, you must take the first possible action from this list: 1) Forage or hunt with one hungry species. 2) If you cannot forage or hunt, starve one hungry population. 3) If you have no hungry population, pass. The Feeding Phase continues around the table until every population has been fed, killed by starvation, or killed in a hunt. Once you pass, you are skipped for the rest of the phase.

walkthrough.title: Foraging
walkthrough.text: When a forager species feeds, it eats plants from the watering hole equal to its size, plus any bonus from traits like Social (+2) or Clawed (+2). Place each eaten plant on top of a hungry population token. A species can never eat more food than it has hungry population. And if the watering hole does not have enough plants, your species only eats what is available. For example, a size 2 species with Social would eat 4 plants, but if there are only 2 plants left, it only takes 2.

walkthrough.title: Hunting
walkthrough.text: A species with a Hunter trait is a hunter. It hunts instead of foraging. To hunt, your hunter must be hungry, its hunt value must be equal to or greater than the prey's defense value, and it must be able to overcome any defense traits like Fast. After a successful hunt: the prey loses 1 population (the prey's controller chooses which), any food on that killed population returns to the supply, and the hunter eats meat from the supply equal to the size of the hunted species. A hungry hunter must hunt if there is legal prey, even if that prey is your own species!

walkthrough.title: Starvation and Extinction
walkthrough.text: If you cannot forage or hunt with any of your species, you must starve one hungry population. Flip it over to show it was killed. It will be added to your new species next round. If the last population of a species dies, that species goes extinct. When a species goes extinct, place its killed population and size dial next to your screen for next round. Return the Hunter card (if any) to the Hunter deck. Return other trait cards to your hand, which you can immediately discard to add plants to the watering hole during this Feeding Phase.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Free Action: Adding Food
walkthrough.text: Before taking any feeding action (foraging, hunting, starving, or passing), you may discard one or more cards from your hand to add plants to the watering hole. Each card has a food value in the bottom right corner. This is a powerful safety valve. If you held cards during the Adapting Phase, you can use them here to add more food when the watering hole runs dry. This can save your species from starvation!

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Game End
walkthrough.text: After the Feeding Phase, remove all plant and meat tokens from your fed population and place them behind your player screen. Each token is worth 1 point at the end of the game. Any leftover plants in the watering hole stay there for next round. Pass the snow leopard to the left. After Round 4, the game ends. Final scoring: 1 point for every plant and meat behind your screen, plus 2 points for each of your surviving species. The player with the most points wins!

summary.title: Game Overview and Goal
bullets: 4 rounds of play
1 point per food token (plant or meat) eaten by your population each round
2 bonus points per surviving species at game end
Tiebreaker: most traits on surviving species

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Watering Hole
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with 3 example players: Alice, Ben, and Carlos. Each will make different decisions so you can see how the Adapting and Feeding Phases play out. Follow along even if you have more or fewer players. Ready? Let's dive in.

walkthrough.title: Preparation: Setting Up Round 1
walkthrough.text: It is the start of Round 1. First, add plants to the watering hole. Let's say the food card showed a 3, and with 3 players that means 9 plants go into the watering hole. Next, each player takes a size 1 species (forager side up) and 1 population token. Finally, each player draws 5 Nature cards. Alice has the snow leopard, so she goes first.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Adapting Turn
walkthrough.text: Alice looks at her 5 cards and decides to build a strong species. She discards 2 cards to add 2 more population to her species, bringing it to 3 population total. Then she plays 1 card face-down as a trait on her species. She still has 2 cards left in hand, which she holds for later. Smart move. More population means more potential points per round, and that trait could be a game-changer.

walkthrough.title: Ben's Adapting Turn
walkthrough.text: Ben decides to go aggressive. He discards 1 card to increase his species' size to 2. Then he takes a Hunter card from the stack as a free action and plays it on his species, flipping the size dial to the hunter side. He also plays 1 card face-down as another trait. He keeps 3 cards in hand. Ben now has a size 2 hunter with 1 population. It can hunt any species with a defense value of 2 or less.

walkthrough.title: Carlos's Adapting Turn
walkthrough.text: Carlos is playing it safe. He discards 1 card to add 1 population to his species (now 2 population). He plays 2 cards face-down as traits. He holds his remaining 2 cards. Carlos is hoping his traits will help him forage efficiently or protect him from Ben's hunter.

walkthrough.title: Reveal Traits
walkthrough.text: Everyone's done with the Adapting Phase, so all face-down traits flip face-up. Alice reveals Social (+2 food when eating). Ben reveals Clawed (+2 when eating, and +2 hunt value). Carlos reveals Plated (+4 defense) and Fast (can only be hunted by species with as many Fast traits). Let's see how this shapes the Feeding Phase!

walkthrough.title: Feeding Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: Now every population starts hungry. Alice has 3 hungry population, Ben has 1 hungry population (his hunter), and Carlos has 2 hungry population. There are 9 plants in the watering hole. Alice goes first since she has the snow leopard.

walkthrough.title: Alice's First Feed: Foraging
walkthrough.text: Alice's species is size 1 with Social, so it eats 1 (size) + 2 (Social) = 3 plants when foraging. She takes 3 plants from the watering hole and places them on her 3 hungry population. All 3 of her population are now fed! The watering hole goes from 9 to 6 plants. Great round for Alice.

walkthrough.title: Ben's First Feed: Hunting
walkthrough.text: Ben's hunter has a hunt value of 4: size 2 plus Clawed +2. He looks around for prey. Alice's species has a defense value of 1 (just her size). Carlos's species has a defense value of 5 (size 1 + Plated +4) and also has Fast. Even though Ben's hunt value of 4 beats Alice's defense of 1, Carlos is safe with his defense of 5. Ben targets Alice's species.

walkthrough.title: The Hunt Resolves
walkthrough.text: Ben's hunter kills 1 of Alice's population. Alice chooses which one to flip over. Since one of her fed population had a plant on it, that plant goes back to the supply. Ouch! Ben's hunter then eats meat from the supply equal to the prey's size. Alice's species is size 1, so Ben takes 1 meat from the supply and places it on his hungry population. Ben's population is now fed.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Mandatory Hunts
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players often make. Ben has to hunt if there is legal prey, even if he would rather not. If Alice's species were the only legal target and it belonged to Ben, he would still be forced to hunt it! A hungry hunter must hunt any legal prey. You cannot choose to starve instead. Always check: is there a species your hunter can reach? If yes, you must hunt it.

walkthrough.title: Carlos's First Feed: Foraging
walkthrough.text: Carlos's species is size 1 with no foraging bonuses, so it eats 1 plant from the watering hole. He takes 1 plant and places it on one of his 2 hungry population. The watering hole drops from 6 to 5 plants. Carlos still has 1 hungry population that needs to eat on a future turn.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Feed
walkthrough.text: Back to Alice. She has 2 remaining population: 1 fed and 1 killed by Ben's hunter. She has no hungry population to feed, and no hungry population to starve. Alice passes! She is done for this Feeding Phase. Remember: killed population do not need food. They are already dead for this round.

walkthrough.title: Ben Passes
walkthrough.text: Ben's only population is already fed with meat. He passes too. Two players have passed, but the Feeding Phase continues until everyone resolves all their population.

walkthrough.title: Carlos's Second Feed
walkthrough.text: Carlos has 1 more hungry population. He forages again, eating 1 plant from the watering hole. Both of Carlos's population are now fed. Carlos passes. The watering hole has 4 plants remaining, which will carry over to next round.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Leftover Food Carries Over
walkthrough.text: New players sometimes clear the watering hole between rounds. Don't! Those 4 leftover plants stay in the watering hole. Next round, you will add the new round's plants on top of them. This can create a feast if food builds up, or lead to desperate competition if a previous round was a feeding frenzy.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Round 1
walkthrough.text: The Feeding Phase is over. Time to score! Each player removes the food tokens from their fed population and places them behind their player screen. Alice had 2 fed population (one was hunted and lost its food), so she scores 2 plants. Ben had 1 fed population, scoring 1 meat. Carlos had 2 fed population, scoring 2 plants. The killed population tokens (Alice's 1) are set aside to be added to new species next round.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Thinking Hunters Score Less
walkthrough.text: Here is another common misconception. Some new players think hunting is weak because Ben only scored 1 point from 1 meat. But remember, meat comes from the supply and is not limited by the watering hole. As Ben adds more population to his hunter, each successful hunt feeds multiple population. A size 3 hunter killing a size 3 prey gets 3 meat in one action. Hunters scale powerfully in the late game.

walkthrough.title: Preparing for Round 2
walkthrough.text: Pass the snow leopard to the left. That means Ben is first player for Round 2. Now for Preparation: add another 9 plants to the watering hole (on top of the 4 leftover, making 13 total). Each player gets a new size 1 species with 1 population. Alice lost 1 population last round, so her new species starts with 2 population instead of 1. Everyone draws 5 more cards.

walkthrough.title: Lost Population Recovery
walkthrough.text: Notice how Alice's lost population came back on her new species. This is a key mechanic in Nature. Population killed by hunting or starvation are never truly gone. They return to you on your new species next round. The same applies to size from extinct species. If your size 3 species went extinct, your new species starts at size 4. This keeps everyone in the game and makes extinction a setback, not a catastrophe.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Population and Defense
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what happened in Round 1. Alice had lots of population but low defense, making her a juicy target for Ben's hunter. Carlos had fewer population but great defense, keeping him safe. The lesson: growing your population without thinking about defense is risky if there are hunters at the table. You need both. A big population with no defense is just a buffet for carnivores.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Holding Cards Is Powerful
walkthrough.text: Remember that Alice held 2 cards during the Adapting Phase. If the watering hole had run dry during the Feeding Phase, she could have discarded those cards to add plants. This free action during feeding is easy to forget but can save your species from starvation. Always consider holding at least 1 card as insurance, especially in rounds with low food.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations!
walkthrough.text: You have walked through a complete first round of Nature. You have seen how the Adapting Phase sets up your strategy, how foraging and hunting work in the Feeding Phase, what happens when population is killed, and how scoring works. You watched Alice build a big population, Ben go predator, and Carlos play defense. Each strategy has strengths and weaknesses.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Remember these three things going forward. First, a hungry hunter must hunt if there is legal prey, so be careful when building a hunter. Second, leftover food in the watering hole carries over between rounds, so plan ahead. Third, lost population and size from extinct species return on your new species, so do not panic if things go badly in one round. Nature is a game of adapting and bouncing back.

walkthrough.title: Before You Evolve
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to grow population versus size versus traits.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every trait and key term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make in Nature. First, putting all your population on one species with no defense. Hunters will feast on you. Second, forgetting that trigger traits are mandatory. Scavenging fires every time any species hunts, not just on your turn. Third, not holding any cards for the Feeding Phase. If the watering hole runs dry, those cards in your hand could save your population from starvation. Fourth, ignoring the size of your species. A size 1 forager only eats 1 plant per feeding, while a size 3 forager eats 3. Size matters!

walkthrough.title: Population vs. Size
walkthrough.text: The fundamental tension in Nature is between growing your population and increasing your size. More population means more potential points per round, since each fed animal scores a food token. But larger size means each foraging action collects more food from the watering hole, and you are harder to hunt. A size 1 species with 5 population needs 5 plants to fully feed, while a size 3 species with 2 population needs the same amount but collects 3 plants per forage. Think of size as efficiency and population as scoring potential.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: Nature is an interactive game. You need to watch what other players are doing during the Adapting Phase. If someone is building a hunter, you need defense. If no one is hunting, you can safely grow population without protection. Count the food in the watering hole and figure out whether there is enough for everyone. If food is scarce, you might want to hold cards to add plants later, or go small so your species does not starve.

walkthrough.title: When to Go Hunter
walkthrough.text: Hunting is powerful but risky. A hunter can feed multiple population from a single kill (meat equals the size of the prey), and meat comes from the unlimited supply rather than the contested watering hole. But hunters must hunt if there is legal prey, even their own species. Go hunter when you see large, undefended species at the table. Avoid hunting if all other species have strong defense traits like Plated or Fast. The worst situation is a hunter with no legal prey that is forced to starve.

walkthrough.title: Trait Synergies
walkthrough.text: Traits are at their best when they work together. Social plus a large size is a foraging machine, eating your size plus 2 additional plants every time you feed. Clawed on a hunter adds +2 hunt value and +2 when eating meat, making your hunter bigger and more lethal. Scavenging on a non-hunter species means it passively gains 2 meat whenever any species on the table hunts, with no risk. Opportunistic works best on species that forage first, since it triggers after foraging if plants remain. Think about how traits combine, not just what each one does alone.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Watering Hole
walkthrough.text: The watering hole is a shared resource, and timing your foraging is crucial. The first player to forage gets the most food. Later players may find the watering hole empty. If you have the snow leopard, you eat first in the Feeding Phase, which is a huge advantage. If you are last, consider holding cards to add emergency food, or build a hunter that does not depend on the watering hole at all. Also remember that leftover plants carry over between rounds. A round with little competition can set up a feast next round.

walkthrough.title: Planning Across Rounds
walkthrough.text: Nature rewards long-term thinking. In Round 1, you have fewer species and cards, so focus on building one strong species that can survive. By Round 3 and 4, you should have multiple species with complementary strategies. Your new species each round is free, and it benefits from any population or size you lost in previous rounds. Do not be afraid to sacrifice a weak species early if it means your new species comes back stronger. The two-point bonus for each surviving species at game end is nice, but not worth keeping a weak species alive at the cost of feeding better ones.

walkthrough.title: Defense Is Underrated
walkthrough.text: New players often focus on offense: growing big populations and hunting aggressively. But defense wins games. Plated adds +4 defense, making even a size 1 species almost impossible to hunt. Fast requires the hunter to have matching Fast traits. Horned punishes hunters by killing their population equal to the Horned species' size. Investing in defense means your population survives, your food stays on them, and you score more points. A species that never gets hunted is a species that keeps scoring every single round.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't pile population on one species with no defense
Trigger traits are mandatory — Scavenging fires on every hunt
Hold cards for the Feeding Phase in case the watering hole runs dry
Don't ignore size — larger species forage more efficiently

summary.title: Population vs. Size
bullets: More population = more scoring potential per round
Larger size = eat more per forage, harder to hunt
Size 1 with 5 population needs 5 plants; size 3 with 2 needs 3 plants per forage
Think of size as efficiency, population as scoring capacity

Count plants in watering hole — is there enough for everyone?
Hold cards if food is scarce

Avoid hunting if all species have strong defense (Plated, Fast)

summary.title: Trait Synergies
bullets: Social + large size = foraging powerhouse (+2 plus size)
Clawed on a hunter = +2 hunt value AND +2 when eating
Scavenging on a non-hunter = passive meat from any hunt
Opportunistic + early foraging = bonus meat while plants last

summary.title: Managing the Watering Hole
bullets: First player forages first = big advantage
Hold cards as emergency food if you forage late
Hunters avoid watering hole competition entirely
Leftover plants carry over — plan for feast-or-famine rounds

summary.title: Planning Across Rounds
bullets: Round 1: build one strong species
Rounds 3-4: multiple species with complementary strategies
Lost population and extinct species' size return on new species
2-point bonus per surviving species is nice but not worth starving others

summary.title: Defense Is Underrated
bullets: Plated (+4 defense) makes even size 1 species nearly unhuntable
Fast requires hunter to have matching Fast traits
Horned punishes hunters by killing their population
Protected species score consistently every round

walkthrough.title: The Herbivore Engine
walkthrough.text: The pure herbivore strategy focuses on maximizing food from the watering hole. Build a large species with Social and high size. A size 4 species with Social eats 6 plants per forage action. With 6 population, that is 6 points in a single feed. The key is getting to the watering hole first. Being first player or having fewer opponents is critical. Pair this with holding cards so you can add emergency plants if the watering hole runs dry. This strategy dominates in low-aggression games with plenty of food.

walkthrough.title: The Apex Predator
walkthrough.text: The hunter strategy aims to bypass the watering hole entirely and feed from the unlimited meat supply. Build a large hunter with Clawed for +2 hunt value and +2 when eating. A size 3 hunter with Clawed has a hunt value of 5 and eats prey size + 2 meat per kill. Target opponents' large species for maximum meat. The downside is that you must hunt if legal prey exists, so you can be forced into bad hunts. Pair your hunter with a small defensive species as backup in case prey dries up.

walkthrough.title: The Scavenger Build
walkthrough.text: Scavenging is one of the most efficient traits in Nature. A Scavenging species eats 2 meat every time any species on the table hunts, regardless of whose turn it is. In a game with multiple hunters, a single Scavenging species can passively collect 4-6 meat per round without ever needing to forage or hunt. The best part is that it stacks. If you have Scavenging on multiple species, each one triggers independently. Combine Scavenging with Opportunistic for a passive feeding machine.

walkthrough.title: The Defensive Fortress
walkthrough.text: If hunters are prevalent, the fortress strategy is devastating. Stack Plated (+4 defense) and Fast on a species. A size 1 species with Plated has a defense value of 5, which is higher than most hunters can reach. Fast means only hunters with matching Fast traits can target you. This species is essentially unkillable, so pile population on it freely. Even if it only eats 1 plant per forage, all that population stays alive and scores every round. Consistency beats flash.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Species Diversification
walkthrough.text: By Round 3, you should have 3 species on the table. Advanced players diversify these species for different roles. One species is the feeder: high size with Social, responsible for foraging lots of food. Another is the defender: low size with Plated and population stacked on it for safe scoring. The third is the opportunist: maybe a hunter or a scavenger that feeds from meat. If one species goes extinct, the others keep scoring. Diversification also means you are never fully locked out by one counter-strategy.

walkthrough.title: Trigger Trait Combos
walkthrough.text: Trigger traits are the advanced player's secret weapon. Scavenging gives 2 meat on any hunt. Opportunistic gives 2 meat after your species forages or hunts, if plants remain in the watering hole. Tusked gives 1 meat when the watering hole empties. You can stack these on one Omnivore species. When your forager eats from the watering hole, Opportunistic fires for 2 meat. When a hunter attacks, Scavenging fires for 2 meat. When the watering hole empties, Tusked fires for 1 meat. That species feeds itself and others without taking a feeding turn.

walkthrough.title: Exploiting the New Species Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Advanced players use the lost population and extinction mechanics strategically. If a species is going to die anyway, let it have as much population as possible, because all that population comes back on your new species next round. A size 3 species that goes extinct gives your new species size 4 to start. This means your new species arrives already huge, ready to forage massive amounts or hunt effectively. Sometimes letting a species die on purpose is the best play if it sets up a monster species next round.

walkthrough.title: Card Economy and Holding Strategy
walkthrough.text: Cards are the most versatile resource in Nature. You can play them as traits, discard them for population, discard them for size, or hold them to add food during the Feeding Phase. Advanced players carefully manage their card economy. In early rounds, invest cards in building species. In later rounds, hold more cards as emergency food. The food value on each card matters when you are deciding what to hold. A card with food value 3 can add 3 plants to the watering hole, which could feed multiple species. Think of held cards as insurance policies.

walkthrough.title: First Player Advantage
walkthrough.text: The snow leopard is more valuable than it looks. The first player feeds first in the Feeding Phase, which means they get first pick of the watering hole before anyone else. With a large, efficient forager, going first can mean eating 4-6 plants before opponents touch the watering hole. Advanced players plan their Adapting Phase around when they will be first player. If you are first player next round, invest heavily in a foraging species this round. If you will be last, lean into hunting or defense.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Optimization
walkthrough.text: In Round 4, the 2-point Game End Bonus for each surviving species becomes critical. Going into the final round, count your species and consider which ones can survive. It may be worth sacrificing population on a doomed species to pump up one that will definitely survive. Keep in mind that every food token behind your screen is 1 point, and every surviving species is 2 points. If you have 3 surviving species, that is 6 bonus points, which can easily swing the final standings. Protect your species in the last round, even if it means scoring fewer food points.

summary.title: The Herbivore Engine
bullets: Size 4 + Social = eat 6 plants per forage
Being first player is critical for watering hole access
Hold cards for emergency plants
Dominates low-aggression games

summary.title: The Apex Predator

Must hunt legal prey — can be forced into bad hunts
Keep a small defensive backup species

summary.title: The Scavenger Build

Stacks across multiple species with Scavenging
Combine with Opportunistic for maximum passive income

summary.title: The Defensive Fortress

Pile population for consistent scoring every round
Consistency beats flash in long games

Feeder (forager), Defender (tank), Opportunist (hunter/scavenger)
If one dies, others keep scoring

Passive meat from hunts, foraging, and empty watering hole
Feeds itself without taking a feeding turn
Stack triggers for exponential passive income

Extinct size 3 species = new species starts at size 4
Sometimes letting a species die sets up a monster next round

May be worth sacrificing food points to keep species alive

entries.definition: Wooden tokens representing individual animals in a species. Each hungry population needs food each round. Fed population score 1 point. Killed population return to you on your new species next round.
One of two food types. Obtained by foraging from the watering hole. Each plant eaten by a population is worth 1 point at game end. Plants are a limited, shared resource.

One of two food types. Obtained from the supply when a hunter successfully hunts, or when trigger traits activate. Each meat eaten by a population is worth 1 point at game end. Meat comes from an unlimited supply.
The central board where plant food is placed each round. The only source of plant food for foraging species. Limited plants are added each round, creating competition among players.

A rotating dial tracking a species' size (1-4 in the base game). One side shows the forager icon, the other shows the hunter icon. Size determines foraging amount, hunt value, defense value, and meat gained when hunted.
A screen used to hide scored food tokens. At the end of each round, food eaten by your population is placed behind the screen. Keeps scores hidden until game end.

The main card deck containing 8 different trait types. Cards can be played as traits, discarded for population, discarded for size, or held to add food during the Feeding Phase. Each card has a food value in the bottom right corner.

A special card taken as a free action during the Adapting Phase. Turns a species into a carnivore. Counts toward the 3-trait limit. Only one Hunter card per species. Flips the size dial to the hunter side.
The first-player marker. Given to the starting player and passed to the left at the end of each round. The first player feeds first in the Feeding Phase, which is a significant advantage.
A population token without any food on it. Hungry population must be fed, starved, or killed during the Feeding Phase. Shown as a bare population token.
A population token with a plant or meat token on it. Fed population score 1 point at the end of the round when food is collected behind the player screen.
A population token flipped over to show it was killed by starvation or hunting. Killed population are added to the player's new species at the start of the next round.
A species without a Hunter card or trigger traits. Herbivores forage plants from the watering hole. This is the default state for every new species.
A species with a trigger trait (like Scavenging, Opportunistic, or Tusked) but no Hunter card. Omnivores forage plants from the watering hole AND eat meat when their trigger traits activate.
A species with a Hunter card. Carnivores cannot forage from the watering hole. They hunt other species for meat instead. Flipping the size dial to the hunter side indicates carnivore status.
A species' attack strength when hunting. Equal to the number on the size dial plus any red-tab bonuses from traits. Must be equal to or greater than the prey's defense value to hunt successfully.

A species' resistance to being hunted. Equal to the number on the size dial plus any grey-tab bonuses from traits. A species can only be hunted if the hunter's hunt value meets or exceeds this number.

A feeding action where a species eats plants from the watering hole equal to its size plus trait bonuses. Each plant is placed on a hungry population token. A species cannot eat more than its hungry population count.

A feeding action where a hunter kills 1 population of a prey species and eats meat from the supply equal to the prey's size. The prey's controller chooses which population is killed. Hunting is mandatory if legal prey exists.
A feeding action taken when a player cannot forage or hunt. Flip 1 hungry population over to show it is killed. The killed population returns on the player's new species next round.
Taken when a player has no hungry population to feed or starve. Once you pass, your turn is skipped for the rest of the Feeding Phase.
An Adapting Phase action. Discard any card from your hand, then take a population token from the supply and add it to any one of your species. No limit to population per species.
An Adapting Phase action. Discard any card from your hand, then rotate one species' size dial to the next number. Maximum size is 4 in the base game.

An Adapting Phase action. Take a card from your hand and play it face-down above a species. Maximum 3 traits per species. Duplicate traits are allowed. Traits are revealed at the end of the Adapting Phase.

A free action during the Adapting Phase. Take a Hunter card from the stack and play it as a trait on a species. Flip the size dial to the hunter side. Counts toward the 3-trait limit. Only 1 Hunter card per species.

A free action during the Adapting Phase. Discard traits from any of your species to make room for new ones. Hunter cards go back to the Hunter deck; other traits go to the discard pile. Removing a Hunter card flips the size dial back to forager.
A free action during the Adapting Phase. Keep 1 or more cards in your hand to use as emergency food during the Feeding Phase or to play in the next round. There is no hand limit.

A free action during the Feeding Phase. Before taking a feeding action, discard cards from your hand to add plants to the watering hole equal to the food value in the bottom right corner of each discarded card.

Occurs when a species' last population is killed or starves. The killed population and size dial are set aside to boost the new species next round. Hunter card returns to the deck; other traits return to hand.
The first phase of each round. Add plants to the watering hole, each player gets a new species (size 1, forager) with 1 population (plus any recovered population/size), and each player draws 5 cards.

The second phase of each round. Each player gets one turn (starting with first player, clockwise) to play cards, take free actions, and evolve their species. Represents millions of years of adaptation.

The third phase of each round. Players take turns feeding species one at a time, clockwise from the first player. Continues until every population is fed, starved, or killed. Represents a snapshot of the ecosystem.
The final phase of each round. Remove food from fed population and place behind player screen. Each food token = 1 point at game end. Leftover watering hole plants carry over.

A trait card. +2 food when eating. If the species is a forager, it eats 2 additional plants. If the species is a hunter, it takes 2 additional meat from the supply when it hunts. No hunt/defense bonuses.
A trait card. +2 when eating meat. Also adds +2 to the species' hunt value (red tab). Effective on hunters because it increases both hunting ability and feeding amount.
A trait card. Defensive trait. This species can only be hunted by a hunter with as many Fast traits. A single Fast makes the species immune to most hunters. No hunt/defense value bonuses from tabs.
A trait card. Adds +4 to the species' defense value (grey tab). Makes the species extremely hard to hunt. A size 1 species with Plated has defense 5, which exceeds most hunters' hunt values.
A trait card. Defensive trigger trait. When this species is hunted, it kills population of the hunter equal to this species' size. Punishes hunters for attacking and can make hunting a losing trade.

A trigger trait (exclamation point). When any species on the table hunts, this species eats 2 meat from the supply. Triggers on any player's turn, not just your own. Mandatory when the condition is met.
A trigger trait (exclamation point). After any of your species forages or hunts, this species eats 2 meat from the supply if there are still plants in the watering hole. Only triggers on your turn.
A trigger trait (exclamation point). When the watering hole becomes empty, this species eats 1 meat from the supply. Triggers on any player's turn. Adds +2 to the species' defense value (grey tab).

Any trait marked with an exclamation point. These have mandatory abilities that activate whenever the trigger condition is met, regardless of whose turn it is. Trigger traits are never optional. The word 'may' would be needed to make one optional.

The number in the bottom right corner of a Nature card. When a card is discarded during the Feeding Phase, this many plants are added to the watering hole. Also used during setup to determine round food amounts.
At the end of Round 4, each player scores 2 points for each of their surviving species. A species survives if it has at least 1 living population at game end.

If players are tied on total points, the player with the most traits on their surviving species wins. If still tied, there is no further tiebreaker (the rulebook suggests ordering pizza and playing again).

Population killed during the Feeding Phase (by hunting or starvation). These are set aside and added to the player's new species at the start of the next round, starting the new species with extra population.

When a species goes extinct, its size is added to the player's new species next round. If a size 3 species went extinct, the new species starts at size 4. Multiple extinct species' sizes combine. Maximum size 4 in base game.
An official variant where all players play their cards during the Adapting Phase simultaneously without looking at opponents' actions. More chaotic, but faster. Recommended for 5-6 player games.
An official variant allowing additional rounds beyond the standard 4. Each extra round adds about 20 minutes. Combined with modules, this can extend Nature into a 2-hour epic game.
Nature is a modular game system. The core game can be customized with location modules (one per game) and other modules (as many as you like). Each combination creates a unique ecosystem experience.
Special cards used only for solo play. Each card represents an AI opponent species with a unique personality. Not used in multiplayer games.
